Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the initial state of level 2:
- Left gray area had a target shape (a small rectangular shape with c/charcoal cells)
- Row 47: w,w,w,w (all off)
- Bottom squares: left blue, right gray
- Left square has yellow at rows 57-58, cols 7-8 (bottom-left)
- Right square has yellow at rows 55-56, cols 23-25 (top-right)
After clicking the left square (first time):
- Target shape disappeared
- Row 47: still w,w,w,w (I think, need to verify)
After clicking the right square:
- Left square: gray, Right square: blue
- Row 47: B,B,B,B (changed from w to B)
After clicking the left square (second time):
- Left square: blue, Right square: gray
- Row 47: w,w,w,w (changed from B to w)
- Target shape still gone
So the pattern is:
1. Clicking the left square (first time) removes the target shape
2. Clicking the right square toggles the left side's bottom vertical bars from w to B
3. Clicking the left square (second time) toggles the left side's bottom vertical bars from B to w
Wait, but I need to reconsider. The bottom squares might control different things:
- Left square: controls the left side buttons
- Right square: controls the right side buttons
But when I clicked the right square, it changed the left side's buttons. And when I clicked the left square, it also changed the left side's buttons. This is confusing.
Actually, let me reconsider the structure. Looking at the bottom squares:
- Left square (blue border): contains a mini representation of the left button area
- Right square (gray border): contains a mini representation of the right button area
The yellow dots in the bottom squares might indicate which buttons should be "on" (B). The left square has yellow at the bottom-left, which could correspond to the bottom row of buttons on the left side. The right square has yellow at the top-right, which could correspond to the top row of buttons on the right side.
But wait, the left square's yellow is at the bottom, and when I click the right square, the left side's bottom vertical bars turn on. And when I click the left square, the left side's bottom vertical bars turn off. This suggests:
- Clicking the left square toggles the left side's bottom vertical bars OFF
- Clicking the right square toggles the left side's bottom vertical bars ON
That doesn't make sense either. Let me think about this differently.
Actually, I think the bottom squares are "selectors" that determine which side is active. When a square is blue, that side is active and its buttons can be toggled. When a square is gray, that side is inactive.
But I clicked on a button (row 33, col 8) when the right square was blue, and nothing happened. Maybe I need to click on the right side buttons when the right square is blue.
Wait, let me re-examine. The left square has yellow dots at the bottom-left, and the right square has yellow dots at the top-right. These yellow dots might be "indicators" showing which buttons should be on. The goal might be to set the buttons to match the yellow dot pattern.
For the left side:
- Yellow at bottom-left → bottom row of buttons should be on (B)
- Currently: row 47 is w (off), row 33 is B (on)
For the right side:
- Yellow at top-right → top row of buttons should be on (B)
- Currently: all rows are w (off)
But I'm not sure how to toggle the buttons. Let me try clicking on the buttons again, but this time on the right side when the right square is blue.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the bottom squares. The left square is currently blue (active). The yellow dots in the left square are at the bottom-left. Maybe the yellow dots indicate which buttons are currently "selected" or "active" on that side. When I click the bottom square, it toggles the corresponding buttons.
Let me try a different approach. Let me look at the relationship between the bottom square's yellow dots and the button states:
- Left square yellow at bottom-left → left side row 47 (bottom vertical bars)
- Right square yellow at top-right → right side row 33 (top horizontal bars)
When I click the left square:
- It toggles the left side's bottom vertical bars (row 47)
- It also removes the target shape (first time only)
When I click the right square:
- It toggles the left side's bottom vertical bars (row 47)
- It also swaps the blue/gray colors of the bottom squares
Hmm, this is getting confusing. Let me try to understand the game by looking at the animation more carefully.
Actually, I think I've been overcomplicating this. Let me step back and think about what the game might be:
1. There are two sides (left and right), each with buttons and a target shape
2. The bottom squares are "selectors" that let me choose which side to work on
3. The goal is to set the buttons to match the target shape
In level 1, the goal was to set all buttons to B. In level 2, the goal might be more complex - perhaps I need to set specific buttons to B based on the target shape.
The target shape on the left side was a small rectangle (4x4 with some inner structure). The target shape on the right side is a U-shape (yellow).
Let me try clicking on the buttons directly to see if they toggle. I'll click on a right side button when the right square is blue.
Actually wait, I just realized something. When I clicked the left square the first time, the target shape disappeared. When I clicked the right square, the target shape didn't reappear. When I clicked the left square the second time, the target shape still didn't reappear. This suggests that the target shape only disappears once, and clicking the bottom squares doesn't toggle it back and forth.
Let me try clicking on the right side buttons to see if they toggle.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me first click the right square to make it blue (active)
# Then try clicking on a right side button
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 56, 'col': 25}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
